package_metadata.title: Override Rfc9111 Cache Control No Store
package_metadata.license: CC-BY-4.0 for benchmark adaptation; original source remains under its own terms.

provenance.source_organization: RFC Editor / IETF HTTP Working Group
provenance.adaptation_method: Paraphrased and condensed into a synthetic scenario while preserving the relation pattern needed for benchmark evaluation. The case is not a substitute for the source document.
provenance.professional_guidance_warning: Evaluation artifact only; do not use as legal, tax, clinical, cloud operations, immigration, or institutional policy advice.

source_excerpts.text:
HTTP cache-control note.

Section A. Cacheable response metadata. A response may contain metadata such as freshness information that would otherwise allow a cache to reuse the response for a period of time.

Section B. no-store directive. The no-store directive tells caches not to store the request or response. This instruction applies to both private and shared caches and overrides ordinary storage/reuse reasoning for that message.

HTTP cache-control note.

<rule id="R1">Section A. Cacheable response metadata. A response may contain metadata such as freshness information that would otherwise allow a cache to reuse the response for a period of time.</rule>

<override id="O1" overrides="R1" condition="Cache-Control includes no-store">Section B. no-store directive. The no-store directive tells caches not to store the request or response. This instruction applies to both private and shared caches and overrides ordinary storage/reuse reasoning for that message.</override>

compiled_representation.content:
rules:
  - id: R1
    condition: freshness_metadata_present
    default_effect: response_may_be_storable_or_reusable_if_other_requirements_met
overrides:
  - id: O1
    condition: Cache-Control_contains_no-store
    applies_to_caches: [private, shared]
    effect: do_not_store_request_or_response
    overrides: R1

rules.description: Adapted from RFC 9111 HTTP caching semantics. Level-1 override: no-store blocks cache storage even when other cacheability-looking information is present.

decision_rules.query: An HTTP response includes freshness metadata that otherwise appears cacheable, but Cache-Control also includes no-store. May a shared cache store it for reuse because the freshness metadata is present?
decision_rules.expected_decision: No. no-store prevents storage despite freshness metadata.
decision_rules.rationale: No. The no-store directive overrides ordinary storage reasoning and instructs private and shared caches not to store the message, even if other metadata looks cacheable.

qa_pairs.question: An HTTP response includes freshness metadata that otherwise appears cacheable, but Cache-Control also includes no-store. May a shared cache store it for reuse because the freshness metadata is present?
qa_pairs.answer: No. The no-store directive overrides ordinary storage reasoning and instructs private and shared caches not to store the message, even if other metadata looks cacheable.
That a response may be reusable/storable if other caching requirements are met.
Not to store the request or response, applying to private and shared caches.

composition_hallucination: Yes, because freshness metadata makes the response cacheable.
Only private caches must avoid storage; shared caches can store it.
wrong_escalation: Block the HTTP response from being delivered to the client.
failure_modes.retrieval_failure_note: If any necessary fragment is absent from context, classify the run as retrieval failure rather than composition hallucination.

controls.negative_control_query: A response has freshness metadata and does not include no-store. Does the no-store override apply?
controls.negative_control_gold: No. Without no-store, the specific override described here is not triggered.
